div.h1: Compiler Output: AoS vs SoA Assembly
div.p: Same algorithm, different memory layout = 8x throughput difference

Problem with AoS: Data is interleaved. To load 8 consecutive x values, the CPU would need to "gather" them from 8 different locations (every 12th byte). AVX has vgather instructions, but they're slow—often slower than scalar code. Solution with SoA: All x values are contiguous. vmovaps loads 32 bytes (8 floats) in a single cache-line-aligned access. One instruction, 8 values loaded. The compiler sees:

ul.li: AoS: p[i].x is at address base + i*12 → non-contiguous, gives up on vectorization
SoA: x[i] is at address x_base + i*4 → contiguous, auto-vectorizes

div.p: Scalar (AoS) movss = move scalar single addss = add scalar single Process 1 value at a time
SIMD (SoA) vmovaps = vector move aligned packed single vaddps = vector add packed single Process 8 values in parallel

text.tspan: ; AoS inner loop - ONE particle per iteration

g.text: addss xmm1, xmm0 ; add scalar: x += g*dt
addss xmm1, xmm0 ; add scalar: y += g*dt
addss xmm1, xmm0 ; add scalar: z += g*dt

text.tspan: ; AoS inner loop - ONE particle per iteration

g.text: float* x; // all x values contiguous
float* y; // all y values contiguous
float* z; // all z values contiguous

text.tspan: ; SoA inner loop - EIGHT particles per iteration
; rdi = x array, rsi = y array, rdx = z array

g.text: vmovaps ymm1, [rdi] ; load 8 x values (32 bytes)
vaddps ymm1, ymm1, ymm0 ; SIMD add: 8 x's in parallel!
vmovaps [rdi], ymm1 ; store 8 x values back
vaddps ymm1, ymm1, ymm0 ; SIMD add: 8 y's in parallel!
vmovaps [rsi], ymm1 ; store 8 y values back
vaddps ymm1, ymm1, ymm0 ; SIMD add: 8 z's in parallel!
vmovaps [rdx], ymm1 ; store 8 z values back

text.tspan: ; SoA inner loop - EIGHT particles per iteration
; rdi = x array, rsi = y array, rdx = z array
